# AI and LLM Agent Instructions for {Project Name}

This document gives LLM agents and code assistants the rules, syntax, patterns,
and known traps needed to use {Project Name} correctly on the first try.

> **Source of truth:** {the code and its godoc/docstrings, or a named spec doc
> such as SPECIFICATION.md}. This manual is *derived* from it — if they ever
> disagree, the source of truth wins.

<!--
HOW TO USE THIS TEMPLATE
- Fill each section; delete sections that genuinely don't apply (don't leave
  empty headers).
- Keep it dense and concrete. Prefer a correct copy-pasteable snippet over prose.
- Every claim here must match the code and the ground-truth spec (no drift).
- Ship this file IN the repo, and make sure it travels with the published
  package (module vendoring / npm "files" / Python package data / crate include).
- Validate it with the clean-agent evaluation (see the guide's evaluation/).
-->

---

## 1. Syntax / API cheat sheet

{The minimum an agent needs to call the thing: the core entry points with exact
signatures, the primary types, and the one canonical "hello world" call.}

```{lang}
{smallest complete working example — imports through a successful call}
```

---

## 2. Crucial rules & constraints

{Hard requirements that are not obvious from signatures. One subsection each.}

### A. {Setup that must happen first}
{e.g. encodings/handlers that must be registered before use, with the code.}

### B. {Constraint}
{...}

### Traps (the places an agent will otherwise guess wrong)
{This is the highest-value content. For each trap: the symptom, the rule, and
the *why*. Examples of trap categories:}
- **Argument order / signature shape.** {If functions differ, give the unifying
  rule and tell the agent to verify signatures rather than copy from a sibling.}
- **Concurrency.** {What is safe to call concurrently; what must not be shared.}
- **Surprising-but-deliberate behavior.** {State it and the reasoning so the
  agent builds the right mental model and doesn't "fix" it.}
- **Build tags / feature flags / modes.** {Anything that changes behavior.}

---

## 3. Recipes (the common real-world patterns)

{For each frequent pattern, a copy-pasteable, known-correct snippet. These
prevent the agent from assembling primitives incorrectly.}

### {Pattern name — e.g. "the most common record/request shape"}
```{lang}
{recipe}
```
{One line on when to use it and any caveat.}

---

## 4. Debugging & error diagnosis

{How an agent should diagnose failures. Name the inspection/trace tools and the
structured error types: which fields they expose (offset, location, field,
expected-vs-actual) and how to match on them.}

```{lang}
{example: catch the typed error, read its location/field, act on it}
```

---

## 5. Validation, performance modes, codegen, etc.

{Any remaining capabilities an agent should know: declarative validation,
performance/build modes, code generation, JSON/schema export. Keep each to the
essentials + a snippet.}

---

## 6. Contributor note

{If agents may modify this codebase, point to AGENTS.txt and state the single
most important invariant inline (e.g. "changes must be mirrored across N code
paths — see AGENTS.txt").}
